mūla.devanāgarī: अनादि-मध्यान्तम् अनन्त-वीर्यम् अनन्त-बाहुं शशि-सूर्य-नेत्रम् | पश्यामि त्वां दीप्त-हुताश-वक्त्रं स्व-तेजसा विश्वम् इदं तपन्तम्
mūla.iast: anādi-madhyāntam ananta-vīryam ananta-bāhuṃ śaśi-sūrya-netram | paśyāmi tvāṃ dīpta-hutāśa-vaktraṃ sva-tejasā viśvam idaṃ tapantam
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 11 (Viśvarūpa-Darśana-Yoga (The Yoga of the Vision of the Universal Form)), verse 19
mūla.speaker: Krishna

word_by_word.grammar: 1st person singular present indicative active verb

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: त्वां दीप्तहुताशवक्त्रं दीप्तश्च असौ हुताशश्च वक्त्रं यस्य तव सः त्वं दीप्तहुताशवक्त्रः तं त्वां दीप्तहुताशवक्त्रम्? स्
। एवंभूतं सर्वस्य स्रष्टारम्? सर्वस्य आधारभूतं सर्वस्य प्रशासितारम्? सर्वस्य संहर्तारम्? ज्ञानाद्यपरिमितगुणसागरम्? आदिमध
। तथा दीप्तो हुताशोऽग्निर्वक्त्रेषु यस्य तम् स्वतेजसा इदं विश्वं तपन्तं संतापयन्तं पश्यामि।
इदं तपन्तम् -- तेजः पराभिभवनसामर्थ्यम्? स्वकीयेन तेजसा विश्वम् इदं तपन्तं त्वां पश्यामि
-- तेजः पराभिभवनसामर्थ्यम्? स्वकीयेन तेजसा विश्वम् इदं तपन्तं त्वां पश्यामि

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If Bhagavān is anādi-madhyānta (without beginning, middle, or end), what does it mean to 'meet' or 'encounter' Him — is encounter possible with what has no temporal boundary?
Moon and sun are read as both cosmic eyes and as grace-and-wrath — does the same perception that blesses one beholder scorch another, and what determines which?
All six schools agree the verse describes infinite potency (ananta-vīrya) — do they agree on what 'potency' means, or are they pointing at different underlying realities?
The fire-face (dīpta-hutāśa-vaktra) appears in the same verse as the cosmic scorching (viśvam tapantam) — is destruction here presented as a flaw, a function, or a form of grace?

Rāmānuja inserts a detailed spatial geometry (limbs extending above and below a single cosmic waist) to resolve the apparent contradiction of one body with infinite limbs — what work does that geometry do theologically?
Madhva grounds the moon-and-sun-eyes in Vedic cosmogony (Puruṣasūkta) — what changes when cosmological description becomes ontological derivation rather than metaphor?
Arjuna says 'I see' (paśyāmi) — is this an act of perception, recognition, or devotional reception, and does your tradition treat those as the same or as stages?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter a situation that seems to have no way out — no beginning of a solution, no middle ground, no ending in sight — that very structure of 'no exit' points to what is prior to all exits: the awareness that contains the situation but is not trapped in it. Practice: when stuck, ask not 'how do I get out?' but 'what in me was never in?'
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: The same Bhagavān whose eyes are moon and sun shows prasāda (cooling grace) to the surrendered and pratāpa (scorching authority) to the resistant. In your relationships, notice that the most loving authority figures — parents, teachers, mentors — do both: their warmth and their challenge come from the same care. Practice kainkarya (service-orientation) rather than self-assertion, and the prasāda register becomes more accessible.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Every capacity you have — intelligence, strength, creativity — is a 'limb born from Him' (tad-aṅga-ja), as the Ṛgveda says of the sun and moon. You did not generate your own abilities; they are derived from Hari's inexhaustible reservoir. Use your talents with the awareness that you are holding them in trust, not ownership. This is not self-deprecation; it is accurate accounting.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha reads the verse as Kṛṣṇa's Kāla-body — time itself is His face. When the day ends, that is the fire-face of evening. When the month turns, that is the moon-eye. When the year burns hot, that is His solar gaze. Living inside Kṛṣṇa's līlā means the rhythms of time are not neutral background noise — they are His prasāda in motion. Practice: greet each transition of time (dawn, dusk, new month) as a moment of His self-disclosure.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's gloss 'utpatti-sthiti-pralaya-rahitam' — free from arising, sustaining, and dissolution — describes Bhagavān, but it also describes what devotion points toward: a relationship that does not depend on favorable conditions arising, good conditions sustaining, or bad conditions ending. Cultivate bhakti that holds steady across all three phases of any life-situation, because the object of bhakti is prior to all three.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana's key move is synecdoche: the infinite arms stand for all infinite limbs. In your contemplative practice, any one quality of Bhagavān — His beauty, His wisdom, His power — is a doorway to the infinite. You do not need to enumerate everything; one attribute, apprehended fully, opens into limitlessness. Begin with the quality of Kṛṣṇa that moves you most, and let the infinite unfold from that single thread.
